description:
The most powerful and feared god of the Aztec pantheon, Tezcatlipoca was the supreme deity, lord of night,
                sorcery, and destiny. His obsidian mirror revealed all secrets and controlled fate. As "He Who Is at the
                Shoulder," he was the invisible, omnipresent force testing humanity through temptation and adversity.

toxcatlFestival.title: The Toxcatl Festival
toxcatlFestival.description: As the supreme god, Tezcatlipoca received the most elaborate sacrifices. The festival of Toxcatl in the fifth month was his greatest ceremony.
toxcatlFestival.ritual: Each year, a young man of perfect beauty was chosen to impersonate Tezcatlipoca for one full year. He was treated as a god made flesh - given fine clothes, trained in music, and attended by servants. For the final month, he was married to four young women representing goddesses. On the day of Toxcatl, he walked alone to a small temple, breaking a clay flute on each step - symbols of the pleasures he was leaving behind. At the summit, he was sacrificed, his heart offered to the very god he had embodied.

formsOfWorship.description: Heart extraction and flaying of victims
Obsidian objects, jaguar pelts, blood, tobacco, rubber balls
Priests used obsidian mirrors to see the future
Jaguar warriors were devoted to his service

cosmology.cardinalDirection: North - realm of darkness and death
cosmology.calendarAssociation: Day sign Acatl (Reed); patron of Toxcatl festival
colors: yellow and black stripes (face paint pattern)

iconography.smokingMirror: An obsidian mirror replacing his foot or worn on his chest, from which smoke emanates - showing visions of the past, present, and future
iconography.jaguarForm: His nahual (spirit animal), representing the night, predatory power, and transformation
iconography.facePaint: Horizontal yellow and black stripes across the face, symbolizing his role as lord of the night sky
iconography.missingFoot: Lost in battle with Cipactli, replaced by obsidian mirror or serpent - showing his sacrifice in creating the world

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of Tezcatlipoca
extendedContent.content:
Tezcatlipoca's origin is shrouded in myth and closely intertwined with the creation of the Aztec universe. According to some accounts in the Florentine Codex and other sources, he was one of the four sons of the primordial creator couple, Ometecuhtli and Omecihuatl, residing in Omeyocan, the highest heaven. He was born fully formed, embodying immense power and potential for both creation and destruction. Tezcatlipoca's birth is less a singular event and more a manifestation of the dualistic forces inherent in the Aztec cosmos. He emerged alongside Quetzalcoatl, representing opposing yet complementary forces, light and darkness, order and chaos. 

These brothers engaged in a cosmic struggle, each taking turns as the sun, marking the successive eras or 'Suns' in Aztec cosmology. Tezcatlipoca's reign as the first sun ended cataclysmically, demonstrating his association with upheaval and destruction. This early role solidified his image as a powerful, albeit volatile, deity. His very existence challenged the notion of a purely benevolent creator god, introducing the concept of a deity capable of both great good and profound evil. His birth, therefore, signifies the inherent instability and cyclical nature of the Aztec universe, where creation is always followed by destruction and renewal.

His name, meaning 'Smoking Mirror,' hints at his ability to see all and his connection to divination and sorcery. This aspect is further emphasized by the tale of his lost foot, often depicted as being replaced by an obsidian mirror or a serpent, symbolizing his power and the sacrifices he made to gain it. This sacrifice also underscores the Aztec belief that power and knowledge come at a cost, reflecting the constant struggle and sacrifice necessary to maintain cosmic balance.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples Dedicated to Tezcatlipoca
extendedContent.content:
Tezcatlipoca, as one of the most powerful and feared gods, received widespread worship throughout the Aztec empire. Temples dedicated to him were prominent in major urban centers, including Tenochtitlan, the capital city. The Templo Mayor, the Great Temple of Tenochtitlan, housed shrines for both Huitzilopochtli and Tezcatlipoca, reflecting their importance in the state religion. Priests dedicated to Tezcatlipoca performed rituals and sacrifices aimed at appeasing him and securing his favor.

Human sacrifice was a significant aspect of Tezcatlipoca's worship, particularly during the Toxcatl festival. A carefully chosen and meticulously groomed young man, embodying Tezcatlipoca's essence, was treated as a living god for a year. He lived a life of luxury and privilege, representing the deity on Earth. However, at the end of the year, he was sacrificed, symbolizing the god's cyclical nature and the renewal of cosmic forces. This ritual, described in detail by Bernardino de Sahagún, highlights the Aztec belief in the necessity of sacrifice to maintain the balance of the universe and ensure continued prosperity.

Beyond human sacrifice, offerings of food, incense, and precious objects were also presented to Tezcatlipoca. The god's priests were skilled in divination and sorcery, acting as intermediaries between the human and divine realms. They interpreted omens and advised rulers on important decisions, further solidifying Tezcatlipoca's influence on Aztec society. The worship of Tezcatlipoca permeated all levels of Aztec life, from the grand ceremonies in the Templo Mayor to personal rituals performed in homes, demonstrating his pervasive power and importance.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation of Tezcatlipoca
extendedContent.content:
Tezcatlipoca's iconography is rich and multifaceted, reflecting his complex nature and diverse roles. He is often depicted with a black stripe painted across his face and a missing foot, typically replaced by an obsidian mirror or a serpent. The obsidian mirror, known as a 'tezcatl,' is a key symbol, representing his ability to see all and his connection to divination. The mirror also symbolizes the reflective nature of reality and the illusory nature of appearances, reminding viewers that things are not always as they seem. The serpent replacing his foot often represents power and transformation.

He is frequently adorned with elaborate headdresses, jewelry, and clothing, signifying his status as a powerful deity. His attire often includes feathers, jade, and gold, materials associated with royalty and divinity. He is sometimes depicted carrying weapons, such as arrows and shields, emphasizing his role as a warrior and protector. His association with jaguars is also significant; he is often depicted wearing jaguar skin or adorned with jaguar motifs, connecting him to power, cunning, and the night.

Codices such as the Codex Borgia and Codex Magliabechiano provide valuable insights into Tezcatlipoca's visual representation. These codices depict him in various contexts, highlighting his role in creation myths, rituals, and divination practices. The details within these representations, such as the specific colors used and the objects he carries, provide clues to understanding his attributes and significance within the broader context of Aztec religion. The visual language used to portray Tezcatlipoca underscores his multifaceted nature, encompassing both benevolent and malevolent aspects.

extendedContent.title: Tezcatlipoca's Role in Cosmic Order and Fate
extendedContent.content:
Tezcatlipoca played a crucial role in maintaining the cosmic order, albeit through often unpredictable and challenging means. As the lord of night and sorcery, he wielded immense power over fate and destiny. He was seen as a force that tested humanity through temptation, adversity, and the constant threat of chaos. His actions, while sometimes perceived as cruel or arbitrary, were ultimately intended to strengthen and refine the Aztec people, demanding resilience and adherence to the established moral code.

His association with the changing seasons and the cyclical nature of time further emphasized his role in the cosmic order. He was not a static or unchanging deity but rather a dynamic force that constantly reshaped and redefined the world. This dynamism is reflected in the stories of his conflicts with Quetzalcoatl, which represent the ongoing struggle between opposing forces that ultimately maintain balance. Tezcatlipoca's influence extended to all aspects of Aztec life, from agriculture and warfare to governance and individual destinies.

The Aztec worldview emphasized the importance of maintaining harmony between the human and divine realms. Tezcatlipoca, as a powerful and capricious deity, demanded respect and appeasement. His influence served as a constant reminder of the fragility of existence and the need for vigilance and sacrifice. While Quetzalcoatl represented the ideals of wisdom and peace, Tezcatlipoca embodied the darker aspects of reality, reminding the Aztecs that life was a constant struggle against adversity and temptation. His role, therefore, was not simply to cause chaos but to challenge humanity and ultimately strengthen their resolve.

Supreme deity rivaling Huitzilopochtli in philosophical importance

primary.name: Templo Mayor (Tenochtitlan) - House of Darkness
primary.description: Inner shrine with obsidian cult objects
primary.findings: Mirror fragments, ritual vessels, deity masks

primary.purpose: Purification and renewal of cosmic order

sacrificialRites.theology: Tezcatlipoca's obsidian mirror required blood offerings to reveal cosmic truths

philosophicalRole.cosmic: Embodied paradox - creation and destruction, order and chaos
philosophicalRole.testing: Tested humans through temptation and adversity
philosophicalRole.fate: Master of tonacayotl (our shared cosmic destiny)

_methodologyNotes: Calendar associations verified against primary codex sources
Codex sources include primary Spanish colonial records and native nahuatl/maya texts
Archaeological sites documented from excavation reports and academic publications
Sacrificial rituals documented from Florentine Codex and validated against archaeological evidence

fourTezcatlipocas.title: The Four Tezcatlipocas
fourTezcatlipocas.narrative: From the primordial duality of Ometeotl came four sons, the Tezcatlipocas, each associated with a cardinal direction and color: Black Tezcatlipoca (north), White Tezcatlipoca/Quetzalcoatl (west), Blue Tezcatlipoca/Huitzilopochtli (south), and Red Tezcatlipoca/Xipe Totec (east). These four creator gods shaped the cosmos and took turns ruling over successive world ages.
fourTezcatlipocas.significance: Establishes the cosmic order of the four directions and world ages

firstSun.title: The First Sun and the Jaguar
firstSun.narrative: Tezcatlipoca became the first sun, ruling over the First World. His reign lasted 676 years until Quetzalcoatl knocked him from the sky with a stone club. Falling into the primordial waters, Tezcatlipoca transformed into a jaguar and devoured all the giants who inhabited that world. Ever since, he has been associated with the jaguar - the night hunter whose spotted coat mirrors the starry sky.
firstSun.significance: Origin of Tezcatlipoca's jaguar association and cosmic enmity with Quetzalcoatl

earthMonster.title: The Earth Monster Cipactli
earthMonster.narrative: In creating the current world, Tezcatlipoca and Quetzalcoatl transformed into giant serpents and tore apart the primordial sea monster Cipactli. From her body they created the earth and sky. But Cipactli remained alive and hungry - to appease her pain and sustain the world, she demanded blood. During this battle, Cipactli bit off Tezcatlipoca's foot, which is why he is often depicted with an obsidian mirror or serpent in place of his missing limb.
earthMonster.significance: Explains the origin of blood sacrifice and Tezcatlipoca's missing foot

fallOfQuetzalcoatl.title: The Corruption of Quetzalcoatl
fallOfQuetzalcoatl.narrative: The most famous myth involving Tezcatlipoca describes his corruption of the virtuous priest-king Quetzalcoatl of Tollan. Jealous of Quetzalcoatl's moral authority and opposed to his ban on human sacrifice, Tezcatlipoca appeared at Quetzalcoatl's court in various disguises. First, he showed Quetzalcoatl his reflection in the obsidian smoking mirror, causing the god to see his own aging face and despair. Then he tricked Quetzalcoatl into drinking pulque until intoxicated. In his drunken state, Quetzalcoatl committed incest with his sister Quetzalpetlatl. Upon waking and realizing what he had done, Quetzalcoatl was so overcome with shame that he abdicated his throne and went into exile.
fallOfQuetzalcoatl.significance: Demonstrates Tezcatlipoca's nature as the lord of change, temptation, and fate - he tests all beings and brings transformation through trials. No one, not even gods, can escape his influence.

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: Tezcatlipoca was often propitiated with human sacrifices, particularly young, handsome warriors who were treated as deities themselves for a year before their ritual death. These sacrifices were believed to appease his power and ensure continued prosperity and cosmic order.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Obsidian
worshipPractices.description: Due to his association with obsidian mirrors, offerings of obsidian objects, such as knives and mirrors, were frequently made to Tezcatlipoca. These offerings were seen as a way to connect with his power of foresight and divination.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.timing: Fifth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: The Toxcatl festival was dedicated to Tezcatlipoca and involved the sacrifice of a young man who had been impersonating the god for a year. The festival included elaborate ceremonies, dances, and feasts to honor the deity.

festivals.name: Monthly Offerings
festivals.timing: Throughout the year, on specific days
festivals.description: Various offerings and ceremonies were performed throughout the year on days associated with Tezcatlipoca in the Aztec calendar. These included burning incense, offering food, and performing rituals to maintain his favor and ward off misfortune.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tezcatlipoca's influence can be seen in modern art and literature that explore themes of duality, destiny, and the hidden aspects of reality. His association with obsidian mirrors and the power of illusion continues to inspire contemporary artists and writers exploring the complexities of human perception and the nature of truth. He remains a powerful symbol of the darker, more mysterious aspects of the human condition.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Hades is the Greek god of the underworld, associated with darkness and hidden knowledge, mirroring Tezcatlipoca's connection to the night and unseen forces. Both deities command respect and fear, wielding power over aspects of life and death.

Odin, similar to Tezcatlipoca, is a powerful and complex deity associated with magic, wisdom, and destiny. He sacrifices an eye for knowledge, echoing Tezcatlipoca's association with seeing the unseen and controlling fate.

Shiva, as the destroyer and transformer, embodies a force of change and power akin to Tezcatlipoca's role in shaping destiny. Both deities represent both creation and destruction, holding dominion over powerful, sometimes terrifying, aspects of the cosmos.
Thoth is the Egyptian god of knowledge, writing, and magic. Like Tezcatlipoca's obsidian mirror, Thoth possesses wisdom and power over secrets, often associated with scribes and recording of destiny.

associations.name: Jaguar
associations.significance: The jaguar represents Tezcatlipoca's power, stealth, and connection to the night.

associations.name: Obsidian
associations.significance: Obsidian is linked to Tezcatlipoca through his mirror, symbolizing divination and hidden knowledge.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black represents the night, the unknown, and Tezcatlipoca's dominion over darkness and shadow.

associations.name: Change
associations.significance: As a trickster and lord of destiny, Tezcatlipoca embodies constant transformation and unpredictable change.

associations.name: Great Bear constellation
associations.significance: The Great Bear constellation symbolizes Tezcatlipoca's role as a celestial warrior, guiding fate and influencing the cosmos.

associations.name: Four
associations.significance: The number four relates to Tezcatlipoca's connection to the four cardinal directions and his overarching power over the world.

associations.name: Sorcery
associations.significance: Tezcatlipoca is the god of sorcery, representing his mastery over magic and the manipulation of reality.

companions.name: Tepeyollotl
companions.description: Tepeyollotl, the god of caves, echoes, and jaguars, is often associated with Tezcatlipoca due to his jaguar form, which is one of Tezcatlipoca's nahuales. He embodies the heart of the mountain and complements Tezcatlipoca's power.

companions.name: Ihuimécatl
companions.description: Ihuimécatl were Tezcatlipoca's servants and messengers. They carried out his will and enforced his decrees in the mortal realm.

companions.name: Titlacauan
companions.description: Titlacauan is another name for Tezcatlipoca himself, representing a specific aspect or manifestation of the god. This association highlights Tezcatlipoca's multifaceted nature and his ability to assume different forms and roles.

companions.name: Macuiltonaleque
companions.description: These were a group of gods associated with pleasure, excess, and games. They are sometimes linked to Tezcatlipoca due to his association with trickery and the unpredictable nature of fate and fortune.

companions.name: Obsidian Mirror
companions.description: Tezcatlipoca's obsidian mirror acts as an extension of his power, allowing him to see all things and control destiny. It is an essential tool and a representation of his omniscience and influence.

shortDescription: Tezcatlipoca, the supreme Aztec deity, was the lord of night, sorcery, and destiny. His obsidian mirror revealed all, testing humanity through temptation.

sources.author: Miller, Mary, and Karl Taube
sources.title: An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to the deities and symbols of ancient Mesoamerica, providing detailed information on Tezcatlipoca's role and attributes.

sources.author: Townsend, Richard F.
sources.title: The Aztecs
sources.description: An authoritative overview of Aztec civilization, offering insights into Tezcatlipoca's significance within the Aztec religious and social structure.

sources.author: Read, Kay Almere, and Jason J. González
sources.title: Mesoamerican Mythology: A Guide to the Myths of Mexico and Central America
sources.description: Explores the complex tapestry of Mesoamerican myths, including the stories and symbolism associated with Tezcatlipoca.
